title: Relief carving primer
subtitle: An introduction to shallow relief carving with gouges and a sloyd knife
excerpt: Relief carving removes material from a flat board to leave a design standing proud of the background. This primer covers the tools, the cut sequence, background removal, and the species that suit beginner relief work.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the relief-carving method and gouge-sweep conventions; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the background-removal sequence.

content.text: What relief carving is

Relief carving works on a flat board. The design is drawn on the face, and the background is cut away until the design stands proud of the surface. The design itself may then be modelled in three dimensions (incising veins, rounding petals, undercutting the edges) or left flat. A shallow relief of 5 to 10 mm below the background surface is manageable for a beginner.
The tools

The primary tools are bench gouges and a sloyd knife. The gouge sweep number describes the curvature of the cutting edge: a No. 3 is nearly flat (a gentle arc), a No. 5 is medium, a No. 7 is deep, and a No. 11 (the veiner) is almost a closed U. Relief carving uses them in this sequence: No. 7 for roughing the background down, No. 5 for cleaning the background to depth, No. 3 for levelling the background surface, No. 11 for veins and fine detail lines.

A carpenter's mallet drives the gouge. It should be beech-headed and heavy enough to drive the gouge in one controlled tap; a light mallet requires multiple taps and the gouge moves between them. Never use a steel hammer on a wooden chisel handle.
The sloyd knife sets the outline cuts and pares the walls of the design against the background. Every relief carving begins with outline cuts from the knife before the gouges are used.
Species for beginner relief carving

Lime is the standard beginner relief-carving species. It is close-grained, cuts cleanly in any direction, and holds fine detail without splitting. Basswood (American lime) is the same species. Cherry carves cleanly but is harder; it is the next step up from lime. Sycamore is good for geometric designs; the interlocked grain is less forgiving for curved forms. Avoid pine for relief carving: the resin channels interrupt cuts and the soft grain crushes under mallet-driven gouges.
The cut sequence
Step 1: Draw the design in pencil on the face of the board. Simple shapes work for a first carving: a single leaf, an oak-leaf cluster, a simple border.

Step 2: Set the outline with the sloyd knife. Work around the pencil line with a near-vertical cut, the blade leaning outward slightly (away from the design) so the knife cut undercuts the design edge. This frees the design from the background and prevents the background cuts from running into the design area.

Step 3: Lower the background with the No. 7 gouge and mallet. Work from the centre of the background area outward toward the outline. Each mallet-driven stroke removes 2 to 3 mm of depth. The gouge travels parallel to the grain direction for the roughing pass.
Step 4: Re-set the outline cut and work the background again with the No. 5 gouge to bring it to the target depth evenly. The No. 5 is used with lighter mallet taps.

Step 5: Level the background surface with the No. 3 gouge in hand-pressure strokes. The No. 3 is nearly flat and removes the gouge facets left by the deeper-sweep tools. Work across the grain direction for levelling cuts.
Step 6: Model the design itself if wanted. Rounded forms use the No. 5 and No. 7 in hand-pressure strokes. Veins use the No. 11 veiner or the tip of the sloyd knife.
Sharpening relief-carving tools

The outer (convex) bevel of a gouge is sharpened on the flat stone using a rolling motion: the gouge rolls on the stone while being drawn down its length, so that the entire bevel width contacts the stone. The inner (concave) face is sharpened with a slipstone: a profiled small stone held in the hand and drawn across the inside radius. A set of slipstones covers the common sweep sizes. Strop outer and inner faces with the leather strop before every session.
A worked example: the single oak leaf

Draw a single oak leaf 80 mm long on a lime board 150 mm wide and 18 mm thick. Set the outline cut with the sloyd knife. Lower the background 6 mm with the No. 7 and No. 5 in three passes. Level with the No. 3. Model the leaf lobes by rounding their edges with light No. 5 hand-pressure strokes, and add the central vein with the No. 11. The finished relief takes about two hours for a first attempt. Sand the background with 320-grit if a fine finish is wanted; leave the knife marks visible on the design modelling for a hand-made quality.
Cross-references
Chip carving uses the sloyd knife alone and works on geometric rather than free-form designs. The chip-carved pine breadboard tutorial shows the method.
Kolrosing works on the finished surface after oiling rather than cutting into the raw wood. The kolrosing technique tutorial covers the method.
